title: Search Engine
text: A search engine is a software system that finds information from a large collection of documents. It builds an inverted index so that queries can be answered quickly instead of scanning every document. Modern engines rank results by relevance using algorithms such as BM25 and PageRank.

title: Inverted Index
text: An inverted index is a data structure that maps each term to the list of documents that contain it. It is the core structure behind full text search, allowing a query to be answered with a fast dictionary lookup rather than a linear scan of the corpus.

title: BM25 Ranking
text: BM25 is a ranking function used to score how relevant a document is to a search query. It rewards term frequency with diminishing returns, boosts rare terms through inverse document frequency, and normalises for document length. It is the default ranker in Lucene and Elasticsearch.

title: PageRank
text: PageRank is an algorithm that ranks web pages by importance based on the link structure of the web. A page is considered important if many other important pages link to it. It was developed by Larry Page and Sergey Brin and became the foundation of Google search.

title: Trie
text: A trie, also called a prefix tree, is a tree data structure used to store strings so that words sharing a common prefix share a path. Tries make it efficient to find every word starting with a given prefix, which powers features like autocomplete and spell checking.

title: Tokenization
text: Tokenization is the process of breaking raw text into smaller units called tokens, usually words. In search, tokenization lowercases text, removes punctuation, and drops common stopwords so that queries match documents consistently.

title: Stopwords
text: Stopwords are very common words such as the, is, and at that carry little meaning for search. Removing stopwords reduces index size and noise, letting the ranking focus on the words that actually distinguish one document from another.

title: Hash Table
text: A hash table is a data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function for near constant time lookup. Python dictionaries are hash tables, and they are what make the inverted index fast to query.

title: Binary Search
text: Binary search is an algorithm that finds a target value in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search range. It runs in logarithmic time, far faster than scanning every element one by one.

title: Quicksort
text: Quicksort is a divide and conquer sorting algorithm that picks a pivot and partitions the array around it, then sorts the partitions recursively. On average it runs in n log n time and is widely used in practice.

title: Graph
text: A graph is a data structure made of nodes connected by edges. Graphs model networks such as roads, social connections, and the links between web pages. Algorithms like breadth first search and depth first search explore them.

title: Breadth First Search
text: Breadth first search explores a graph level by level, visiting all neighbours of a node before moving deeper. It finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph and is a building block of many other algorithms.

title: Dynamic Programming
text: Dynamic programming solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing the results to avoid recomputation. Classic examples include the knapsack problem, edit distance, and the longest common subsequence.

title: Machine Learning
text: Machine learning is a field of artificial intelligence where systems learn patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed. It powers recommendation systems, image recognition, language models, and modern search ranking.

title: Neural Network
text: A neural network is a model loosely inspired by the brain, made of layers of connected units that transform inputs into outputs. Deep neural networks with many layers drive advances in vision, speech, and natural language processing.

title: Natural Language Processing
text: Natural language processing is the branch of artificial intelligence concerned with how computers understand and generate human language. It includes tasks such as tokenization, translation, sentiment analysis, and question answering.

title: Database Index
text: A database index is a structure that speeds up data retrieval at the cost of extra storage and slower writes. B trees and hash indexes let a database find rows without scanning the entire table, much like an inverted index in search.

title: Caching
text: Caching stores the results of expensive operations so that future requests can be served faster. Search engines cache popular queries and their results to reduce latency and load on the ranking system.

title: Distributed Systems
text: A distributed system spreads computation and data across many machines that coordinate over a network. Large search engines shard their index across thousands of servers so that no single machine holds the entire web.

title: Sharding
text: Sharding splits a large dataset into smaller pieces called shards that live on different servers. A search index is sharded so queries can run in parallel across machines, and the partial results are merged before ranking.

title: Latency
text: Latency is the time it takes for a system to respond to a request. Search engines obsess over latency because users abandon slow results, so they cache, shard, and precompute aggressively to keep responses fast.

title: Web Crawler
text: A web crawler is a program that systematically browses the web to discover and download pages for indexing. It follows links from page to page, respecting rules in robots files, and feeds the content into the search index.

title: Relevance
text: Relevance measures how well a document satisfies a user's query. Good search ranking balances relevance signals such as term matches, freshness, popularity, and the quality of the source to put the best result first.

title: Vector Search
text: Vector search represents documents and queries as numerical embeddings and finds matches by measuring distance in that vector space. It captures meaning beyond exact keywords, so a query can match a document that uses different words.

title: Compression
text: Compression reduces the size of data so it takes less space and transfers faster. Search engines compress their inverted index heavily, encoding document id lists as small gaps so a huge index fits in memory.
